planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to average, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; lance-leaved loosestrife grows naturally in moist woodland openings, stream banks, and meadow edges across eastern North America. It spreads by rhizomes and self-sows to form loose colonies.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in moist woodland borders, stream banks, and rain gardens; lance-leaved loosestrife is a slender, upright native perennial with narrow, lance-shaped leaves and cheerful, star-shaped yellow flowers in midsummer. It is one of the more adaptable native loosestrifes, tolerating somewhat drier conditions than the wetland species while still preferring consistent moisture. The flowers provide nectar for small native bees, including Macropis bees that are specialists on Lysimachia pollen and oil. The plant spreads steadily by rhizomes and seeds to form naturalistic colonies in appropriate moist, shaded areas, combining naturally with native ferns, sedges, and woodland wildflowers. A native alternative to the highly invasive purple loosestrife (Lythrum salicaria) for moist areas, though unrelated botanically.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring or fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full shade; hairy spicebush grows naturally in wet, shaded pocosins, bay swamps, and moist pine flatwoods of the Carolinas coastal plain. It is rarer and more restricted in distribution than common spicebush (L. benzoin).

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full shade in naturalistic woodland or wetland settings; hairy spicebush is a rare and threatened native shrub of the Carolinas coastal plain, naturally restricted to wet, shaded pocosins and bay swamps where it grows beneath pond pines and Atlantic white cedars. Like common spicebush (L. benzoin), the leaves, bark, and twigs are aromatic when crushed with a spicy, benzoin-like fragrance. The small yellow flowers appear on bare stems in very early spring, providing pollen for early-emerging native bees. A host plant for spicebush swallowtail butterfly (Papilio troilus) caterpillars, which use both Lindera species. More restricted to wet, acidic, shaded conditions than common spicebush and more appropriate for pocosin and bay swamp restoration. A rare plant with significant conservation value.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in very moist, highly acidic, peaty or sphagnum soil in full shade; creeping snowberry requires extremely specific conditions — cool, wet, acidic, shaded bogs or mossy, acid conifer forest floors in the boreal zone. It does not tolerate heat, dryness, or neutral to alkaline soils.

directions: Plant in very moist, highly acidic, peaty or sphagnum-based, humus-rich soil in full shade in cool, humid climates zones 2–6; creeping snowberry is a delicate, thread-like native evergreen groundcover of boreal bogs, mossy conifer forests, and cool, acid swamps across Canada and the northern United States. The tiny, rounded, glossy evergreen leaves form a fine mat just 1–3 inches tall, and the tiny white berries that form in late summer are pure white, edible, and have the characteristic wintergreen flavor of the Gaultheria genus. They were collected and eaten by indigenous peoples and early settlers as a minor food source. Like all Gaultheria, the leaves contain methyl salicylate (oil of wintergreen) and have been used as a flavoring. This species requires very specific, cool and perpetually moist conditions and is not appropriate for most garden settings, but excellent for recreating boreal bog gardens in the northern states and Canada.

planting.note: Plant small rooted divisions in spring in very well-drained, dry to moist, highly acidic, humus-rich, needle-duff soil in full shade; pipsissewa grows symbiotically with mycorrhizal fungi in conifer forest duff and is notoriously difficult to transplant successfully — disturb the roots as little as possible and never use garden soil.

directions: Plant in dry to moist, highly acidic, humus-rich, conifer-needle duff soil in full shade beneath pines or oaks; pipsissewa is a small, semi-woody, evergreen native of dry conifer and mixed forests across the Northern Hemisphere, recognized by its whorled, dark green, glossy, toothed leaves with pale midribs and its nodding clusters of waxy, pink-white, fragrant flowers in summer. Like spotted wintergreen (C. maculata) and other Pyrola relatives, it is mycoheterotrophic — partially dependent on mycorrhizal fungi for nutrition and extremely difficult to establish in garden conditions without intact fungal partners. The plant has a long history of use in traditional Native American medicine and was an ingredient in the original commercial root beer formulas. Do not collect from wild populations. Best appreciated as a feature of intact native forest rather than a cultivated garden plant.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall at 3–4 inches depth in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; wood lily is one of the few native lilies that holds its flowers upright (facing the sky) rather than drooping. It requires good drainage and benefits from light shade in warmer zones.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; wood lily is one of the most striking native wildflowers of eastern and central North America, producing upward-facing, brilliantly orange to orange-red, spotted flowers on relatively short stems — unlike the drooping, turk's-cap form of most other native lilies. The upright orientation makes wood lily a natural hummingbird flower, and ruby-throated hummingbirds are among its most important pollinators. The plant is increasingly rare in the wild due to deer browsing, loss of fire-maintained habitat, and illegal collection. In the garden, wood lily requires excellent drainage and is short-lived in rich, moist conditions. Plant in sandy soil on a slope or raised bed with consistent but moderate moisture and light afternoon shade in warmer zones.
